http://www.springer.com/series/7651For over 35 years, biological scientists have come to rely on the research protocols and
methodologies in the critically acclaimed Methods in Molecular Biology series The series was
the ﬁrst to introduce the step-by-step protocols approach that has become the standard in all
biomedical protocol publishing Each protocol is provided in readily-reproducible step-by-
step fashion, opening with an introductory overview, a list of the materials and reagents
needed to complete the experiment, and followed by a detailed procedure that is supported
with a helpful notes section offering tips and tricks of the trade as well as troubleshooting
advice These hallmark features were introduced by series editor Dr John Walker and
constitute the key ingredient in each and every volume of the Methods in Molecular Biology
series Tested and trusted, comprehensive and reliable, all protocols from the series are

It is our distinct pleasure to present the second edition of MiMB Mitochondrial Medicine to
the ever increasing number of scientists and physicians who are as fascinated by this tiny
organelle as we are We started working on the ﬁrst edition in September 2014 and were able
to bring about one year later two volumes with a total of 70 chapters to the market As of
today (July 2020), 195K downloads have been recorded for Volume I1and 90K downloads
for volume II2 In light of the rapidly growing and expanding ﬁeld of Mitochondrial
Medicine, we readily accepted the invitation to compile a second edition, which we started
to work on in March 2019 This second edition as offered here involves a total of 88 chapters
with 45 of them written by new contributors who were not part of our ﬁrst edition The ﬁrst
and second editions combined subsequently present work from 115 mitochondrial labora-
tories from around the globe We therefore believe these ﬁve volumes combined to be the
most comprehensive source of know-how in the wide-ranging ﬁeld of Mitochondrial
Medicine Dividing 87 chapters equally over three volumes proved to be a bit challenging We
chose the subtitle Targeting Mitochondria for volume I, Assessing Mitochondria for volume
II, and Manipulating Mitochondria and Disease Speciﬁc Approaches for volume III while of
course being well aware of signiﬁcant overlaps between these three areas of research For
example, it is quite obvious that mitochondria are being targeted for the purpose of either
assessing them or to manipulate them We therefore ask all authors not to be too critical
regarding the placement of their particular chapter The reader we believe will anyway
choose to download a chapter of his/her interest quite independently of its placement in
one of the three volumes

mitochondria.protocol:
1. **Introduction to Mitochondrial Medicine:** The article discusses the second edition of the book "Mitochondrial Medicine," which focuses on the study of mitochondria and its role in disease. The book contains a total of 88 chapters, with 45 new contributors.

2. **Purpose of the Second Edition:** The second edition was compiled to provide comprehensive knowledge in the field of Mitochondrial Medicine. The three volumes of the book are divided into different areas of research: Volume I focuses on targeting mitochondria, Volume II on assessing mitochondria, and Volume III on manipulating mitochondria and disease-specific approaches.

3. **Volume III Content:** Volume III, titled "Manipulating Mitochondria and Disease-Specific Approaches," contains 87 chapters that cover various methods and approaches for manipulating mitochondria and studying their role in specific diseases.

Based on the information provided, there is no specific mention of a detailed protocol for mitochondrial isolation. The article mainly provides an overview of the book and its contents.
mitochondria.organisms: No organisms mentioned in the Mitochondrial Extraction Protocol.

summary: Summary 4: Mitochondrial Medicine, Vol. 3: Manipulating Mitochondria and Disease-Specific Approaches, 2nd Edition, is part of the Methods in Molecular Biology series, edited by Volkmar Weissig and Marvin Edeas. This comprehensive resource, with contributions from 115 mitochondrial laboratories worldwide, focuses on targeting, assessing, and manipulating mitochondria for disease-specific approaches. The book, indexed in PubMed, offers step-by-step protocols, detailed procedures, tips and tricks, and troubleshooting advice. It is published by Springer Science+Business Media and is a valuable tool for researchers in the pharmaceutical sciences. The series is edited by John M. Walker, affiliated with the School of Life and Medical Sciences at the University of Hertfordshire in Hatfield, the Department of Pharmaceutical Sciences at Midwestern University in Glendale, and Cochin Hospital, Cochin Institute, and INSERM U1016 in Paris. His work is published in the Springer Nature journal, which is indexed in PubMed. The book "Targeting Mitochondria" edited by John M. Walker provides a comprehensive source of methodologies, step-by-step protocols, materials and reagents, tips and tricks, and troubleshooting advice for assessing and studying mitochondria. The book is available online with the ISBN 978-1-0716-1270-5 and can be accessed at http://www.springer.com/series/7651.

article:
Chunk # 1.
All chapters in these three volumes were written for graduate students, postdoctoral
associates, independent investigators in academia and industry as well as physicians by
leading experts in their particular ﬁeld We are extremely grateful to them for having
found the time to either update their chapter from the ﬁrst edition or to write a new chapter We will not forget that for many if not all of our contributors the worldwide COVID-19
pandemic posed additional and unexpected hurdles towards ﬁnishing their manuscript in
due time Thank you to all The idea for our original book proposal leading to the ﬁrst edition of MiMB Mitochon-
drial Medicine originated in our efforts to organize a series of annual conferences on
Targeting Mitochondria ( www.targeting-mitochondria.com ), the tenth one of which mean-
while has taken place in November 2019 in Berlin, Germany Due to the ongoing pandemic,
our 11th conference (October 29–30, 2020) will be a virtual one but we are sure it will not
be less exciting than all the previous editions 1https:/ /link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-1-4939-2257-4  2https:/ /link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-1-4939-2288-8  vLast but not least we would like to sincerely thank John Walker, the series editor of
Methods in Molecular Biology , for having invited us to compile this second edition and for his
unlimited guidance and help throughout the entire process We also owe sincere thanks to
Patrick Marton, the Executive Editor of the Springer Protocol Series, for always having been
available in assisting us throughout the entire project Glendale, AZ, USA Volkmar Weissig

summary:
**Iteration 3:**

Initial Summary: MiMB Mitochondrial Medicine is a three-volume book written by leading experts in the field. It targets graduate students, postdoctoral associates, investigators, and physicians. This second edition includes updated and new chapters. The book originated from annual conferences on Targeting Mitochondria. Despite the challenges of the COVID-19 pandemic, the 11th conference will be held virtually. The authors express gratitude to the series editor, John Walker, and the executive editor, Patrick Marton, for their invaluable guidance.

Revised Summary: MiMB Mitochondrial Medicine, a comprehensive three-volume book, is written by leading experts in the field. It targets graduate students, postdoctoral associates, investigators, and physicians. This second edition includes updated and new chapters that cover a wide range of topics in mitochondrial medicine. The book originated from annual conferences on Targeting Mitochondria and despite the challenges of the COVID-19 pandemic, the 11th conference will be held virtually. The authors express their sincere gratitude to the series editor, John Walker, and the executive editor, Patrick Marton, for their invaluable guidance and support throughout the entire process.

summary:
Iteration 6:

Summary: Ida Annunziata, Jason Andrew Weesner, and Alessandra d’Azzo are experts in mitochondrial diagnostics, specifically in using a discovery-based biochemical platform for phenotyping human peripheral blood cell mitochondria. Margaret A M Nelson and Kelsey H Fisher-Wellman conduct bioenergetic profiling to understand the energy metabolism of human pluripotent stem cells. Gizem Inak, Marie-Thérèse Henke, and Alessandro Prigione develop integrative methods for studying cardiac energetics. Philippe Diolez, Véronique Deschodt-Arsac, Guillaume Calmettes, Gilles Gouspillou, Laurent Arsac, Pierre Jais, Michel Haissaguerre, and Pierre dos Santos isolate mitochondria from retinal pigment epithelial cell cultures and apply the high-resolution respirometric assay (XFe96 Seahorse assay) to analyze their function. Masaaki Ishii, Gyda Beeson, Craig Beeson, and Bärbel Rohrer perform deep sequencing of mitochondrial DNA for further study. Gabriel Arismendi-Morillo investigates the ultrastructure of the mitochondria-associated membranes in human tumor specimens.

Entities: 
1. Mitochondrial Diagnostics: A discovery-based biochemical platform for phenotyping human peripheral blood cell mitochondria.
2. Bioenergetic profiling of human pluripotent stem cells.
3. Integrative methods for studying cardiac energetics.
4. Isolation of mitochondria from retinal pigment epithelial cell cultures and an application of high-resolution respirometric assay (XFe96 Seahorse assay).
5. The isolation and deep sequencing of mitochondrial DNA.

topics.topic: Mitochondrial Diagnostics
topics.justification: The experts mentioned in the summary are specifically focused on using a discovery-based biochemical platform for phenotyping human peripheral blood cell mitochondria.

topics.topic: Bioenergetic Profiling of Human Pluripotent Stem Cells
topics.justification: Margaret A M Nelson and Kelsey H Fisher-Wellman conduct bioenergetic profiling to understand the energy metabolism of human pluripotent stem cells.

topics.topic: Integrative Methods for Studying Cardiac Energetics
topics.justification: Gizem Inak, Marie-Thérèse Henke, and Alessandro Prigione develop integrative methods for studying cardiac energetics.

topics.topic: Isolation of Mitochondria and High-Resolution Respirometric Assay
topics.justification: Philippe Diolez, Véronique Deschodt-Arsac, Guillaume Calmettes, Gilles Gouspillou, Laurent Arsac, Pierre Jais, Michel Haissaguerre, and Pierre dos Santos isolate mitochondria from retinal pigment epithelial cell cultures and apply the high-resolution respirometric assay (XFe96 Seahorse assay) to analyze their function.

topics.topic: Isolation and Deep Sequencing of Mitochondrial DNA
topics.justification: Masaaki Ishii, Gyda Beeson, Craig Beeson, and Bärbel Rohrer perform deep sequencing of mitochondrial DNA for further study.

topics.topic: Ultrastructure of Mitochondria-Associated Membranes in Human Tumor Specimens
topics.justification: Gabriel Arismendi-Morillo investigates the ultrastructure of the mitochondria-associated membranes in human tumor specimens.

hypothetical_questions.question: How can mitochondrial diagnostics using a discovery-based biochemical platform help in phenotyping human peripheral blood cell mitochondria?
What are the potential benefits of bioenergetic profiling in understanding the energy metabolism of human pluripotent stem cells?
Imagine a scenario where integrative methods are successfully used to study cardiac energetics. How could this knowledge be applied to improve cardiovascular health?
How does the isolation of mitochondria from retinal pigment epithelial cell cultures and the application of high-resolution respirometric assay contribute to the analysis of their function?
How might the isolation and deep sequencing of mitochondrial DNA contribute to our understanding of genetic diseases and potential treatments?

modeling is of critical importance as mutations in the mitochondrial genome give rise to a variety of
pathological conditions and play a contributing role in many others Nuclear localization and transcription
of mtDNA genes followed by cytoplasmic translation and transport into mitochondria (allotopic expres-
sion, AE) provide an opportunity to create in vivo modeling of a targeted mutation in mitochondrial genes Accordingly, such technology has been suggested as a strategy for gene replacement therapy in patients
harboring mitochondrial DNA mutations Here, we use our AE approach to transgenic mouse modeling of
the pathogenic human T8993G mutation in mtATP6 as a case study for designing AE animal models Key words Allotopic expression, ATP6, Transgenic mouse, mtDNA, Mitochondrial disease, Animal
modeling
1 Introduction
Throughout the course of eukaryotic evolution, genetic material
from mitochondria has translocated and taken residence within the
nuclear genome [ 1] Replicating this phenomenon in the labora-
tory by expressing a mitochondrial gene in the nucleus and target-
ing the resultant protein to the mitochondrion is termed allotopic
expression (AE) (Fig 1), ﬁrst coined in the late 1980s [ 2] AE has
multiple uses as a research tool in studying topics ranging from
mitochondrial genomic evolution to human disease arising from
mutations in mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) Creation of animal models of AE has potential value in studying
several aspects of mitochondrial physiology [ 3] These include
probing human mtDNA genetic pathology, the limits of the evolu-

summary: Revised Summary (Iteration 3): Researchers from Johnson V A Medical Center and the Department of Neurosciences at the Medical University of South Carolina discuss the concept of allotopic expression (AE) as a strategy for modeling mitochondrial diseases in mice. AE involves expressing mitochondrial genes in the nucleus and targeting the resulting proteins to the mitochondria. They focus on the AE modeling of the T8993G mutation in mtATP6, which is associated with mitochondrial diseases. Animal models of AE, such as those developed by HANNAH ROSA from the Department of Diabetes at King's College London, SABINE SCHMITT from the Institute of Toxicology and Environmental Hygiene at the Technical University of Munich, and PAUL M. SMITH from the Department of Paediatrics at the Royal Aberdeen Children’s Hospital, have potential value in studying mitochondrial physiology and can be used for preclinical trials of interventions for mitochondrial diseases. These animal models are important for understanding human mtDNA genetic pathology, the limits of the evolutionary transfer of mtDNA to the nucleus, mitochondrial protein import, mechanisms of aging, and gene therapy. The AE-based gene therapy modality has shown promise in clinical trials for treating Leber’s Hereditary Optic Neuropathy (LHON), an ocular disease caused by mtDNA mutation in the ND4 gene. In vivo AE models, such as those developed by NATASCHA SOMMER from the University of Giessen and Marburg Lung Center, RALF STEINBORN from the Genomics Core Facility at VetCore, University of Veterinary Medicine Vienna, and MADHAVEE THUMIAH-MOOTOO from the Department of Cellular & Molecular Medicine at the University of Ottawa, can be used for preclinical trials of interventions targeted at mitochondrial diseases. These models can also contribute to the development of gene replacement therapy for patients with mitochondrial DNA mutations. Other researchers, such as ALYAVENYAMINOVA from the Laboratory of RNA Chemistry at the Institute of Chemical Biology and Fundamental Medicine SB RAS in Novosibirsk, and ANTO´N VILA-SANJURJO from the Departamento de Bioloxı ´a at the Universidade da Corun ˜a in A Corun ˜a, Spain, have focused on the molecular mechanisms of AE and its potential applications in treating mitochondrial diseases. The AE models developed by KEN-ICHI WADA from the Bioengineering Laboratory at the RIKEN Cluster for Pioneering Research in Wako, Japan, and JASON ANDREW WEESNER from the Department of Genetics at St Jude Children Research Hospital in Memphis, USA, have provided insights into the genetic pathology of mitochondrial diseases and the effectiveness of AE-based gene therapy. Additionally, YEKATERINA YATCHENKO from the Laboratory of Psychobiology at the B Rappaport Faculty of Medicine, Technion IIT in Haifa, Israel, and ME´LY YVES from the UMR 7021 - Laboratory of Biophotonics and Pharmacology (LBP) at the University of Strasbourg/CNRS in Illkirch, France, have contributed to the understanding of mitochondrial physiology and the potential therapeutic applications of AE. The AE models developed by HANS ZISCHKA from the School of Medicine at the Technical University of Munich and the Helmholtz Center Munich, German Research Center for Environmental Health in Neuherberg, Germany, have provided valuable insights into the mechanisms of mitochondrial protein import and the pathogenesis of mitochondrial diseases. The authors highlight the importance of AE animal models for advancing our understanding of mitochondrial diseases and developing effective interventions.

topics.topic: Allotopic expression (AE) as a strategy for modeling mitochondrial diseases in mice
topics.justification: The summary discusses the concept of AE and its use in modeling mitochondrial diseases in mice.

topics.topic: AE modeling of the T8993G mutation in mtATP6
topics.justification: The summary specifically mentions the AE modeling of the T8993G mutation in mtATP6 as a focus of the research.

topics.topic: Animal models of AE for studying mitochondrial physiology and preclinical trials
topics.justification: The summary highlights the potential value of animal models of AE in studying mitochondrial physiology and conducting preclinical trials for interventions in mitochondrial diseases.

topics.topic: AE-based gene therapy for treating Leber's Hereditary Optic Neuropathy (LHON)
topics.justification: The summary mentions the use of AE-based gene therapy in clinical trials for treating LHON, an ocular disease caused by mtDNA mutation.

topics.topic: In vivo AE models for preclinical trials and gene replacement therapy
topics.justification: The summary discusses the use of in vivo AE models for preclinical trials of interventions targeted at mitochondrial diseases and the development of gene replacement therapy for patients with mitochondrial DNA mutations.

topics.topic: Molecular mechanisms of AE and its potential applications in treating mitochondrial diseases
topics.justification: The summary mentions researchers focusing on the molecular mechanisms of AE and its potential applications in treating mitochondrial diseases.

topics.topic: Insights into the genetic pathology of mitochondrial diseases and the effectiveness of AE-based gene therapy
topics.justification: The summary highlights the insights provided by AE models into the genetic pathology of mitochondrial diseases and the effectiveness of AE-based gene therapy.

topics.topic: Understanding mitochondrial physiology and potential therapeutic applications of AE
topics.justification: The summary mentions researchers contributing to the understanding of mitochondrial physiology and the potential therapeutic applications of AE.

topics.topic: Mechanisms of mitochondrial protein import and the pathogenesis of mitochondrial diseases
topics.justification: The summary mentions the insights provided by AE models into the mechanisms of mitochondrial protein import and the pathogenesis of mitochondrial diseases.

topics.topic: Importance of AE animal models for advancing understanding and developing interventions in mitochondrial diseases
topics.justification: The summary emphasizes the importance of AE animal models for advancing our understanding of mitochondrial diseases and developing effective interventions.

hypothetical_questions.question: How does allotopic expression (AE) in animal models contribute to our understanding of mitochondrial diseases and potential interventions?
What are the key advantages and limitations of using AE-based animal models for studying mitochondrial physiology and preclinical trials?
Imagine a future where AE-based gene therapy is widely available for treating mitochondrial diseases. How might this impact the field of medicine and the lives of patients?
How does the research on AE animal models complement the studies on molecular mechanisms of AE and its potential applications in treating mitochondrial diseases?
As a healthcare professional, how would you explain the concept of allotopic expression (AE) and its potential implications to a patient with a mitochondrial disease?
